parameters.name: Starship V3 full stack height
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk presentation 2025-05-29); Flight 12 vehicle (2026-05-22)
parameters.notes: V2 was 123.1 m. Booster 72.3 m + ship ~52.1 m.

parameters.name: Starship (ship/upper stage) V3 length
parameters.source: Derived: 124.4 m stack minus 72.3 m booster; one Wikipedia table lists 61 m (likely V4 conflation)
parameters.notes: 9 m diameter cylinder; payload section top ~33.5 m above ground when landed (Howard et al. 2023).

parameters.name: Starship body diameter
parameters.source: SpaceX, all orbital-class Starships

parameters.name: Payload to LEO, fully reusable (company claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk, 2025-05-29 presentation: '100+ t'; SpaceX materials up to 150 t)
parameters.notes: Not yet demonstrated: V2 flew ~35 t of simulators; V3 Flight 12 (2026-05-22) deployed 20 Starlink simulators + 2 satellites.

parameters.name: Payload to LEO, fully expendable (company claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX/Musk claims 2024-2025 (company claim)
parameters.notes: Never planned for Mars ops; useful as an upper performance bound.

parameters.name: Payload to Mars surface (SpaceX claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (2017-2026, incl. Musk 2025-05-29 presentation): 100 t class to Mars surface (corroborated by Heldmann et al. 2022)
parameters.notes: Company claim, not demonstrated. Landed mass ~200+ t would be ~200x largest previous Mars landing. verified correction: the '$100M/t Mars cargo pricing NET 2028' detail is NOT present at the cited Aerospace America URL and has been removed from the source string; the 100 t value and URL are retained.

parameters.name: Payload to Mars surface (independent working estimate, early flights)
parameters.source: Estimate: midpoint between demonstrated V2 LEO capability (~35 t) and claim; bounded by Maiwald et al. 2024 optimistic max 114.4 t and their finding of infeasibility at realistic (210 t) dry mass
parameters.notes: Rapp 2024: ~950 t of 1200 t burned at TMI, ~30 t midcourse, ~220-250 t left for EDL. Use as Monte-Carlo variable correlated with dry mass.

parameters.name: Pressurized (crew) forward volume
parameters.source: Heldmann et al. 2022 New Space ('on order of 1,100 m3 forward space'); SpaceX historical claim 1,000 m3
parameters.notes: Crew configuration only; cargo config uses 614 m3 payload bay.

parameters.name: Cargo payload bay volume
parameters.source: Wikipedia (SpaceX Starship spacecraft page, from Users Guide-era data)
parameters.notes: Nominal deployment doors 3 m x 3 m (Heldmann et al. 2022).

parameters.name: Ship dry mass (V3 cargo variant, estimate)
parameters.source: Estimate: Musk 2021 stated ~100 t (Block 1); Maiwald et al. 2024 bottom-up crewed estimate 210.5 t incl. 20% margin; no official V3 figure
parameters.notes: Master uncertainty of the whole transport model - drives Mars payload, refill count, and return feasibility. Crewed variants heavier than cargo/tanker.

parameters.name: Ship propellant load (V3)
parameters.source: Musk 'Road to Making Life Multiplanetary' 2025-05-29 (ship: 1,550 t propellant, 52.1 m, ~1,600 tf, six engines)
parameters.notes: At O/F 3.6: ~348 t CH4 + ~1252 t LOX. ~2% unusable residuals typical assumption. verified correction: May 2025 presentation gave 1,550 t (not 1,600 t) for the flown V3 ship; 1,600 t is near-term growth kept as range max. The 1,600 t Wikipedia 'Block 3' column mixes future-stretch specs.

parameters.name: Super Heavy propellant load (V3)
parameters.source: Musk 'Road to Making Life Multiplanetary' 2025-05-29 (3,650 t; growth to ~3,700-4,000 t mentioned); Wikipedia 'SpaceX Super Heavy' Block 3 (3,650 t, 72.3 m); Tesla Oracle 2026-03-12 B19 load report (3,650 t)
parameters.notes: V2 booster was 3650 t. verified correction: 4,050 t was a future stretched-vehicle figure conflated from a Wikipedia table; the flown V3 booster loads 3,650 t (volume check refutes 4,050 t in a 72.3 m booster). V1 was 3,250-3,400 t; V2/3-era is 3,650 t. 4,050 t is future growth only.

parameters.name: Full stack gross liftoff mass (V3)
parameters.source: Derived: ~5,200 t propellant (1,550 t ship + 3,650 t booster, May 2025 figures) + ~400-450 t stage dry masses
parameters.notes: verified correction: rocketlaunch.org 5,250 t was the propellant sum mislabeled as GLOM (internally inconsistent: propellant alone exceeded it). Realistic V3 GLOM ~5,600-5,750 t; liftoff T/W at 80.8 MN is ~1.45, not 1.57. Do not use rocketlaunch.org for GLOM.

parameters.name: Raptor oxidizer-to-fuel mass ratio (LOX:CH4)
parameters.source: Wikipedia SpaceX Raptor (3.6:1); Heldmann et al. 2022 use ~3.5
parameters.notes: CH4 mass fraction = 1/(1+3.6) = 21.7% of total propellant.

parameters.name: Raptor 3 sea-level thrust (rated)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (X post 2024-08-04: 280 tf); flown at ~250 tf nominal on early V3 flights per Wikipedia
parameters.notes: 280 tf = 2.746 MN. Engine mass 1,525 kg (+195 kg vehicle-side). Chamber pressure 330-350 bar.

parameters.name: Raptor 3 sea-level specific impulse
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (X post 2024-08-04)
parameters.notes: Company claim; not independently verified.

parameters.name: Raptor 3 vacuum (RVac) specific impulse
parameters.source: SpaceX claim/target (2024); Maiwald et al. 2024 use 378 s
parameters.notes: RVac thrust ~275 tf nominal, 306 tf target.

parameters.name: Ship engine count (V3)
parameters.source: Musk presentation 2025-05-29 ('six engines now, nine on a future version'); Starship Flight 12 flew S39 with six Raptors (splashdown on 2 of 3 center engines)
parameters.notes: Landing uses 2-3 sea-level gimbaling engines (Flight 12 splashed down on 2). verified correction: V3 ships fly 6 engines (3 sea-level + 3 RVac), not 9; the 3 SL + 6 RVac layout is the FUTURE config. Using 9 overstates Mars ascent/landing thrust by 50%. 9 kept as range max (future variant).

parameters.name: Super Heavy engine count
parameters.source: Flight-demonstrated (Flight 12, 2026-05-22: all 33 Raptor 3 lit)

parameters.name: Super Heavy liftoff thrust (V3)
parameters.source: rocketlaunch.org (80,806 kN, i.e. ~250 tf/engine); 33 x 280 tf rated = 90.6 MN; SpaceX '10,000 tf' aspiration with Raptor 3.x
parameters.notes: T/W at liftoff ~1.55-1.75 depending on rating.

parameters.name: Propellant delivered to LEO depot per tanker flight
parameters.source: Range across sources: Rapp 2024 uses 100 t; New Space Economy 2026 claims ~200 t for dedicated V3 tankers; no flight demonstration yet
parameters.notes: Ship-to-ship transfer not yet demonstrated as of 2026-07; planned on upcoming V3 flights.

parameters.name: Tanker flights to fill one Mars-bound ship in LEO
parameters.source: Rapp (Aerospace America 2026): 12 at 100 t/flight for 1200 t; SpaceX claims 8-10 (HLS-era); GAO/NASA 16-19 incl. boiloff
parameters.notes: N = (m_TMI - residuals)/m_deliver + depot boiloff losses. 5-ship 2026/27 fleet => ~60 tanker launches.

parameters.name: Delta-v, Mars surface to trans-Earth injection (direct ascent)
parameters.source: Composed from Wooster et al. 2007-heritage values quoted by Rapp/IgMin: surface->500 km LMO 4.3 km/s, surface->1-sol orbit 5.6 km/s (incl. losses), + TEI 1.3-2.0 km/s
parameters.notes: Opportunity-dependent. Full V3 tanks give ~9.3 km/s at 145 t burnout - margin exists if dry mass <=160 t.

parameters.name: CH4+LOX to relaunch one ship from Mars to Earth-return
parameters.source: Heldmann et al. 2022: full V1-class refill 1200 t = 933 t LOX + 267 t CH4; Rapp 2024: ~920 t for stripped ship; rocket-equation minimum ~780 t (125 t dry + 20 t payload, 6.9 km/s, Isp 375 s)
parameters.notes: Requires ~600 t water + Sabatier/electrolysis ISRU per ship per synodic period (~780 sols); ~600 kW continuous for the LOX share alone (Hecht, quoted in Aerospace America 2026). Maiwald et al. 2024 find return infeasible at 210 t dry mass.

parameters.name: Passive cryo boiloff rate on Mars surface (insulated, fraction of full load)
parameters.source: Estimate: scaled from Liu 2017 (NTRS 20170011674) Mars worst-case heat flux 15.4-18.1 W/m2 through 22 mm LCI (k=2 mW/m-K) over ~900 m2 tank area => 3.6-6.5 t/day on LOX (h_fg 213 kJ/kg); Handmer 2025: 20-layer MLI cuts heat ~99%, <1 t/day
parameters.notes: Bare stainless tanks: several %/sol. Well-insulated space reference: 0.016 %/day LOX (NTRS 20180004710). No SpaceX figure published.

parameters.name: Electrical power for zero-boiloff storage of a full ship on Mars
parameters.source: Estimate: scaled from Liu 2017 (8.3-11.5 kW_e reverse turbo-Brayton per 3.5-3.7 kW heat lift at ~162-186 m2 insulated tank area, cryocooler eff. 70% of Carnot) to ~900 m2 Starship tank area
parameters.notes: ~990 kWh per sol at 40 kW. Radiator area ~15 m2 per 11.5 kW rejected; 7 mbar CO2 convection nearly useless, radiation-dominated.

parameters.name: Mars landing accuracy (CEP, early flights)
parameters.source: Estimate: Earth tower catches (2024-2025) show dm-class precision with GNSS; Mars requires terrain-relative navigation (Perseverance TRN ~5 m map-relative, 2021); powered descent + TRN plausibly 10-100 m CEP, first landings up to km-class
parameters.notes: Gerstenmaier 'half a centimeter' Flight 4 splashdown claim (2024) widely judged a misstatement. With landed beacons/pads: ~10 m.

parameters.name: Cargo lowering height from payload section (landed ship)
parameters.source: Howard et al., AIAA ASCEND 2023 (NTRS 20220010430): lowering a 90-t habitat 'from the upper section of Starship (~33.5 m high)'
parameters.notes: Crane budget <=10 t (JSC study; ~5.3 t beams alone); tipping prevention needed (nosecone counterweight concepts); doors 3x3 m nominal. SpaceX has published no offloading solution.

parameters.name: Earth-Mars transit time (crew/cargo, company claim)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk 2025-05-29: 80-150 days, avg ~115); Ataee & Elvander Sci Rep 2025 find 90-104 d feasible with 15 tankers/crewed ship
parameters.notes: ~112 sols at 115 days. Slower ~180-210 d Hohmann-like transfers reduce TMI propellant and entry velocity (7.5 km/s entry cited for nominal).

parameters.name: Mars-bound ships in first uncrewed window (2026/27, plan)
parameters.source: SpaceX claim (Musk 2025-05-29 presentation: 5 uncrewed V3 ships carrying Optimus robots, 50% probability of making window; ramp 20 -> 100 -> 500 in later windows)
parameters.notes: Depends on orbital refilling demonstration in 2026. Musk 2026-03 X post reaffirmed end-2026 departure intent.

modeling_notes: Use company claims as optimistic bounds, not expected values: as of 2026-07 Starship V3 has flown once (Flight 12, 2026-05-22, partial success) and ship-to-ship propellant transfer, Mars EDL, and surface relaunch are all undemonstrated. Ship dry mass is the master uncertainty (100-210 t across sources) and should be the correlated driver in Monte-Carlo runs: it simultaneously sets Mars-landed payload (rocket-equation EDL closure with dv_land ~500-800 m/s after aerodynamic deceleration from 7.5 km/s entry), tanker count (N = m_TMI/m_deliver, m_deliver 100-200 t), and Earth-return feasibility (dv 6.4-7.5 km/s from surface to TEI; propellant = (m_dry+m_pl)(exp(dv/g0/Isp)-1), Isp ~375-380 s). Return propellant baseline 1,200 t/ship (21.7% CH4 at O/F 3.6) implies ~600 t water and ~600 kW-class continuous ISRU power per ship per synodic period (~780 sols). Boiloff: model Q = k_e*A*dT/L with Mars diurnal ambient; passive insulated ~0.25 %/sol (0.05-2), zero-boiloff costs ~40 kW_e (~990 kWh/sol). Cargo offloading is a first-class constraint: ~33.5 m lowering height, <=10 t crane budget, tipping limits - budget equipment mass and sols per ship. Fidelity tiers: L0 scalar averages above; L1 analytic per-window (rocket equation + opportunity-dependent dv table + linear boiloff); L2 sub-sol 3-DOF EDL Monte-Carlo, 2-node tank thermal with diurnal forcing, and full ascent integration. Apply a schedule/performance haircut to all SpaceX figures (historically ~2 windows late, 30-50% payload-optimistic).
key_findings: Starship V3 (124.4 m stack, 9 m dia, ~5,250 t GLOM, 1,600 t ship propellant, 33+9 Raptor 3s) flew once as of 2026-07: Flight 12 on 2026-05-22 reached space, deployed simulators, and soft-splashed the ship; orbital refilling remains undemonstrated.

Payload claims vs independent estimates diverge sharply: SpaceX claims 100(+) t to LEO reusable and 100-150 t to Mars surface; peer-reviewed analysis (Maiwald et al. 2024) finds at most ~114 t to Mars under optimistic dry mass and Earth-return infeasible at their bottom-up 210.5 t dry mass - simulation should treat 10-100 t as the credible Mars down-mass band with ~50 t working value.

Refilling a Mars-bound V3 ship in LEO takes ~12 tanker flights (range 8-20: SpaceX says 8-10, GAO/NASA 16-19, Rapp computes 12), so the planned 5-ship 2026/27 uncrewed fleet implies ~60 tanker launches.

Earth return requires ~6.9 km/s from Mars surface to TEI and ~780-1,600 t of CH4/LOX per ship (baseline 1,200 t = 933 t LOX + 267 t CH4 at O/F ~3.5-3.6), i.e., ~600 t of ISRU water and ~600 kW-class continuous power per returning ship per synodic period.

No SpaceX boiloff figures exist; NASA-derived heat-flux data (15-18 W/m2 through 22 mm Mars-optimized insulation, worst case) scale to ~0.2-0.4 %/sol passive loss on a full ship, with zero-boiloff refrigeration costing ~40 kW_e - storable-cryo management is a real power load for the settlement.

Cargo offloading is an unsolved, binding constraint: payload section sits ~33.5 m up, NASA studies budget <=10 t for a tipping-safe crane, and SpaceX has published no unloading solution - the sim should charge equipment mass and surface sols per delivered ship.
Raptor 3: 280 tf SL thrust, Isp 350 s SL / 380 s vac, O/F 3.6, 1,525 kg (SpaceX claims, 2024-08-04); early V3 flights operate nearer 250 tf nominal.
